label: Rylands v Fletcher
scenario_patterns: A factory stores large quantities of chemicals that leak and contaminate neighbouring land
A construction company's water reservoir bursts and floods adjacent properties
An industrial gas tank ruptures, causing toxic fumes to escape onto neighbouring premises
A swimming pool on an upper floor of a building leaks and causes damage to units below
legal_tests: The defendant brought or accumulated something onto their land
The thing accumulated amounts to a non-natural use of the land
The thing was something likely to do mischief if it escaped
The thing did in fact escape from the defendant's land to the plaintiff's land
The escape caused foreseeable damage to the plaintiff's property or land
common_fact_patterns: Chemical or hazardous material storage at industrial premises that leaks into neighbouring land
Water escape from reservoirs, tanks, or swimming pools causing flooding to adjacent properties
Gas or fuel storage facilities where leaks affect surrounding areas
Construction site accumulations (water, slurry, soil) escaping to neighbouring properties
Waste or pollutant accumulation in industrial zones that contaminates surrounding land
sg_case_references: Tesa Tape Asia Pacific Pte Ltd v Wing Seng Logistics Pte Ltd [2006] SGHC 36 — Rylands v Fletcher principles applied to water escape in Singapore
Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 2297 v Seasons Park Ltd [2005] SGHC 144 — non-natural use and escape in strata property context
Jurong Town Corp v Sembawang Engineers and Constructors Pte Ltd [2009] SGHC 44 — accumulation and escape from construction works
Cambridge Water Co v Eastern Counties Leather plc [1994] 2 AC 264 — English authority regularly applied in SG for foreseeability of damage requirement
party_roles: plaintiff/occupier of land affected by the escape
defendant/occupier who accumulated the thing on their land
defences: Act of God — the escape was caused by natural forces that could not reasonably have been anticipated
Act of a stranger — the escape was caused by the deliberate act of a third party over whom the defendant had no control
Consent — the plaintiff consented to the accumulation (e.g., common benefit)
Statutory authority — the accumulation was authorised or required by statute
Default of the plaintiff — the plaintiff's own act or default caused the escape
